speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March 29, 1996. Interview with Fred F. Roer, born as Fritz Roer. Interviewer, Janice Englehart in Seattle, Washington, the United States. This interview will be conducted in English. My name is Janice Englehart. Today is March 29, 1996. I will be conducting an interview with Mr. Fred Roer in Seattle, Washington. Mr. Roer, can you begin by stating your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Fred F. Roer. Live in Seattle now for, what is, 46 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: At birth my name was Fritz Roer. And I was born in Kerpen, which is a little city near Cologne in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: On what day?

speaker: interviewee
text: On November the 2nd, 1920. And I lived there till [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think beginning of 1939, spring of 1939. And then we were forced out of our-- of the home we were living in and moved to a so-called Juden apartment, which is a Jewish apartment just for Jews which was shared by one other family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- that was in Cologne. That's in the [INAUDIBLE] 63-- yeah, 63. And we moved from a, let's see, three-- five bedroom home into a one bedroom apartment where we had to share the kitchen and bathroom with the other family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you get to that point, can you tell us about Kerpen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kerpen was a town between 5,000 and 6,000 people. Everybody knew each other. My family on my mother's side grew up in this town as far back as I can go, I would say about 1500, 1600s-- in the 1500s, 1600s. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- my mother's father was a cattle dealer and after he passed away in 1913, his sons took over the business. My parents were married in 1914 in Cologne the year-- less than a year before my grandfather passed away. We lived there like everybody lives here in America. There's no-- there was no difference between Jews or non-Jews. There-- there was a little Jewish community of about 30 people-- 30 families, approximately 100 people. And we had a synagogue which was run Orthodox. Most of the people were between Orthodox and conservative. And also we had a one-room day school from grades one through eight, and the teacher was also the rabbi and the cantor. He ran the whole synagogue. And we would go to services every Friday night and every Saturday and Saturday all day in the-- in the afternoon. And special occasions would be-- every holiday would be celebrated. And we lived about-- in a town which was I would say about close to 98% Catholic, and there were some-- some Protestants there. The rest was Jews, but nobody even cared about which religion you were. Everybody was your-- you have friends with everybody else. The main interest for us kids at that time was soccer, playing in the-- in the forest nearby, catching squirrels, jumping from tree to tree like everybody-- every [? squirrel ?] did. And all my friends-- most of my friends were all non-Jews. On the soccer team we were with one other Jewish boy and the coach was Jewish, but nobody even saw any different or any-- made any point out of it. My dad was-- my dad was elected to the city council which was a volunteer project. He died in an accident in 1928 in Cologne. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he had-- when he got married, he opened in this little town in Kerpen a-- a business in conjunction with his-- with his dad who had-- had the business in a town called [? Bruhl ?] which was about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eight to 10 miles away from-- from Kerpen. So the business were run together. And after my dad passed away, my mother jumped in there and my grandfather kept the business going because-- with the-- with the intention that my brother and myself would take it after awhile, after we are old enough. My brother was five years older than myself. His name is-- was Herman, and he was killed in a-- in a concentration camp as-- as my mother did. My grandfather just died shortly before the-- the evacuation started. Coming back to Kerpen, we-- we lived, like I said, a normal life. We were respected. We were I would say a middle class. We had a-- we lived in a house with five bedrooms and-- and living room, dining room, two kitchens-- one for-- one for the summer, one for the winter. And we had also the business itself, the showroom at the same house. And then in the back of it there were the-- the warehouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a business was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The business was a so-called gas and water [NON-ENGLISH]. That is an electric. It was a gas and water and electric [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Installation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Installation. And also we-- we had a lot of appliance sales, appliances, light fixtures, and close to what you would call a hardware store here or more like an appliance store. And-- and both towns, and it was one business. At the height of the business when my dad was alive, we employed about 20 journeymen. When-- up to 1939 when Hitler came, there were at least 10 to 15 journeymen always working for this business. And my-- my mother and my grandfather ran the business together, was one business. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] like I said, we were just middle class, and the main thing was soccer. I played on the soccer team when I was seven years old. The soccer team over there was not like here in the States. There was one sport organization for the whole town. And from the youngest kids on till the seniors, they had a team for each group, each level of age-- age-- age level. Like I said, I played on it was when I was seven until '33 when I was all of a sudden kicked out of the game-- out of the soccer team. Schooling. I went to-- for four years first with this Jewish day school that I mentioned before. From there I went to the so-called gymnasium. If you wanted to go to college, you had to have gymnasium education. And the gymnasium was all-- not like here, the high school. The gymnasium was private and it was actually a preparation for-- for college. And I went there for four years, and that was in '33, '34. Then I was kicked out of that school because I was Jewish. Like I said before, we lived a normal life. There was no different between the kids or between religions. In fact, an example how we were treated in the town-- it's the best example is that there was a-- a parade in the town, and I had a-- a very bad-- I was sick for over a month in a hospital. And after I came out, they rerouted the parade by our house so I could watch the parade.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you can see how-- how well we were liked there. I come back later on-- when I come to the Kristallnacht, I will show a other example how-- how close everybody was and how close we were liked.

speaker: interviewee
text: The business was very good, what you would call very good. We had a maid but which was at that time in Germany no big deal-- a lot of people did. The maid got a very minimal salary. She lived in with us, and she was treated like one of the family. She never would be eating in the kitchen or something. She always would eat with us together. And this-- this was just part of the family. And we-- I remember two maids, we had and each one of them until '35 and Hitler came out with the Nuremberg Laws. You couldn't have any maids that age anymore, the young girls. Both of the maids were with us for over 10 years until they got married. So they were treated like a daughter. My mother really treated any-- any girl like anybody else. The business was 60 years old and never had a lawsuit until the Nazi time when some guy pulled some trick on doubling receipts on a radio he bought. And even the judge-- he was represented for 40 years by a-- by a high Nazi-- high Nazi official who was the-- the-- his lawyer, and even the judge come-- came to the conclusion that there was nothing illegal done.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he declared it a mistrial because he-- this Nazi said, you can't believe a Jew. You have to believe the other one. But the judge knew exactly who-- who it was but he was in no position to judge it. So this is how the life was over there. Were-- were most of your friends Jewish or did you have Jewish and non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had Jewish-- I had non-- a non-Jewish friend but I would say 98% of the friends were non-Jewish. And I-- I was at their home, and they were at our home. We were living together, playing together. Whole time there was no-- like I said, there was no different. We were very well-liked. Like I said, my parents grew up. My mother's friends were all more or less all non-Jewish. And her parents lived in that town and grew up there. Her great-- great-- my great-grandparents lived there. Their parents lived in that town. So on my mother's side, the whole-- as far back-- like I said, before I can go lived in that town and were always very respected.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Until 1933, life was a normal life like any place else. And in the summertime, we went swimming in the river. In the wintertime, we went-- near the river, to-- to-- the [INAUDIBLE] would flood-- flood with water and then it would freeze over and we would ice skate in there. But this was just a normal thing. And a big thing was having a bicycle, which everybody had, especially me because I just took it out from the business. We sold them. So as a little boy, every time I grew a little bit, a bigger bicycle was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was-- and then naturally like I said, the main entertainment was soccer. That was all day long. So what happened in '33? How did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1933, all of a sudden, everything changed. The kids which were playing with me all of a sudden didn't say hello to me-- some of them. Some did. Some of them, especially one, who went to school with me at the gymnasium, he all of a sudden started not talking to me, making troubles, making slight remarks. Other boy who played soccer on the same team than I did, for instance, pushed me off the sidewalk and said the Jews have to walk in the street. A boy I grew-- my age, we grew up together, and from one week to the other or from one day to the other, he changed. He put on a big [INAUDIBLE] uniform, and he thought he was-- he was already the king of Germany. This is how the things changed. Then in the beginning of '33 after [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hitler took over, the Jews didn't think he would last for long because every few months there was a different government in Germany at that time because the recession and everything. He promised them the world, and they went for him and voted for him. He-- everybody knows about the burning of the Reichstag there. He blamed the Communists for doing it. He did it-- his-- his hordes did it themselves just to get the sympathy and to get everybody in line. Then in beginning of, I think, March or April, around this time, they started boycotting Jewish businesses. There would be one in a brown shirt would stand in front of the business and would say, don't buy here because it's a Jew. They would write Jew on the-- on the show window with a-- with a star of David. And this was actually the beginning of-- of the-- that you would feel the Nazi deal. But even in this little town, people would-- lots of people would ignore it and the business still was going quite well. We still had six, seven, or eight-- maybe eight employees. And until about I would say beginning of '38, middle of '37, our business wasn't that much affected. My brother was an apprentice in-- in-- in Cologne and not in the business itself because my mother didn't want him to be the boss over the-- over the employees. Then in '34 after I was kicked out of school, I went also into apprenticeship in plumbing in-- in Cologne which involved a-- a train ride where you had to transfer a couple of times of about an hour or less, a little less. And even on that train ride, a lot of boys or friends would still talk to me, sit with me. And we would play cards to-- to keep busy to get there. One-- then after awhile one of the big Nazis who took the same train every morning that I took, came over and told the boys they couldn't sit there with me playing cards anymore. I had to sit in the last wagon of the train. And the boys moved-- some of them moved with me there to show support, but he harassed them so much that they later on gave up on it. In fact, one of the boy who still tried to stay with me all of a sudden he got this notice to go into the army. He was drafted because-- I'm quite sure because of that, that he didn't follow the Nazi line there. And then also and I remember being on the train and always being in the last wagon, being harassed on-- by this Nazi. And then one day, all of a sudden, they burned all the books, I think was also '33 or '34. All the books of the Jewish artists, all the books what didn't compile with the Nazi ideas. The books of all these-- these big Jewish authors like Heine, Mann, Einstein. Then the books written by socialists. In fact, the-- the US Constitution were burned because the big universities-- the university made a big show of it. The fraternities there, each had a special different uniform. The Germans were big on uniforms, so they made a big parade out of them, a big bonfire, and burned all them books. And now they were-- would be happy to have them back because it is manuscripts in there, but never can be replaced. This was things what was going on. Then in 1935, these so-called Nuremberg Laws. You couldn't marry non-Jews anymore. You had to-- the race laws, the so-called race laws. You couldn't have any maids anymore. The maids had to be at least 40 years old if she were to be a maid in a Jewish place or it had to be a Jewish girl. And this when really the harassment started. The doctors couldn't practice anymore. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Excuse me. [DRINKS] The Jewish doctors couldn't practice anymore, the lawyers couldn't anymore. And only for Jewish people they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this period, what-- what do you recall? What was your mother saying? What were you discussing in your family? Was there any talk about trying to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not until-- real-- real pressure to start leave-- to leave came after '38 after the Kristallnacht. But I had one uncle who lived in [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] is the part of Germany what was separated from the main country by the corridor for the Polish-- for Poland which had-- which gave Poland a-- a road towards the ocean, to the-- to Danzig-- Gdansk the Poland called it-- to-- to have access to the sea. And he lived up in that part, and this part was very anti-Semitic and very Hitler. And he was already beaten in 1934. They beat him up there. When he came back to Kerpen where he was born, and he, his wife, and two children. And he lived in Kerpen where-- like I said before, the anti-Semitists were there, but it wasn't that intense because all them people grew up there and everybody knew each other, like I said before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were a certain amount of anti-Semitism. There was a certain amount of Nazis there, but they-- they weren't as-- as-- as brutal as they were over in [INAUDIBLE]. And he was then the first one-- he said when he-- in '37 when it got still started getting worse he said, I'm not gonna stay. We're going to get out of here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- he left Germany. In the beginning of-- of '38, he and his family [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] moved or immigrated to Colombia, South America. And this was the first one of the family. Now, my mother was thinking of immigrating too, but she had two young boys, a widow who-- who had a business what was still fairly good. After my brother completed his apprenticeships, he-- he was in the business already taking over, so everything was still going. And I was still a young kid. I was 16, 17. And so she-- the idea was to immigrate after I completed my education after my brother got established. And so the urgency for-- to immigrate didn't start till 1938 after the Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe how you experienced Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Kristallnacht I experienced-- first of all when it started I was in Cologne. I was in the apprenticeship. And this company I was working for was also naturally a Jewish company because I couldn't work for anybody else. And at that time, in the early afternoon when it started, I left work and went to the train to go home. To be sure that I wasn't too harassed, I left the train one station before Kerpen and walked, which was about two and a half miles, walked to home through the back ways because I didn't know what was going on in Kerpen. When I got home around 4, 5 o'clock, everything was still fairly quiet. Then all of a sudden the hordes of the Nazis came, the drunks came, [INAUDIBLE] all drunks. The first thing they did, they tore the synagogue part. They took out the benches and put them in the middle of the street. They tore the-- the prayer shawls to pieces and put everything in one pile and made a big bonfire there. The interesting part, which I didn't know at that time, is when I came back after the war to Germany, one priest had saved one Torah. He had grabbed it and took it away without knowing-- anybody knowing it. When he gave it back to me after I came back, that was the first Torah we had in Cologne to start over the Jewish community. But then they burned the Torahs, the other ones. They burned everything in that place. Not the building itself because there were caretakers living in there which was not Jewish. So-- but the other things they made a big bonfire in front of the synagogue. And then the mob started going from business to business, from house to house where Jews lived. They [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went through some places like-- like a tornado hit the house. The businesses were-- were damaged and all torn apart, looted. We were lucky. I don't know why or what. In our house, only one show window was broken and the door was-- was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] damaged with an axe. That was all what happened to us. But my grandma and my aunt-- to-- old lady in her 60s with her unmarried daughter lived in a house where they grew up with-- with, let's see, three-- five children. A big house. And the mob went through that house like a tornado hit the whole house. There wasn't one thing in-- in this house-- damaged--
